remaining_tasks.name: GSD Quick: unify architecture — remove serve, move trickle into MCP lifespan, single DotMDService

decisions.decision: Target architecture: single process (MCP HTTP), trickle lives in MCP lifespan, serve removed entirely
decisions.rationale: REST API never used externally. Two DotMDService instances = 2x memory (~200MB wasted). MCP is the access plane, core logic belongs in one process.

decisions.decision: CLI surface kept as-is — not removed
decisions.rationale: Might be needed in the future, zero cost to keep, just not used in prod

decisions.decision: Docker healthcheck: add /health to FastMCP app (port 8080) instead of rely on serve
decisions.rationale: FastMCP runs uvicorn — can add a health route directly to the Starlette app

next_action: Run /gsd-quick to unify architecture: (1) check how FastMCP exposes Starlette app for custom /health route, (2) add /health to mcp_server.py, (3) move trickle lifespan from api/server.py into mcp_server.py using FastMCP lifespan parameter, (4) update start.sh to only run mcp, (5) update Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK port 8000→8080, (6) rebuild image, (7) restart container, (8) verify healthy + trickle running in logs.
context_notes: Current container 'dotmd' is running and healthy with start.sh starting two processes (mcp bg + serve fg). The unification makes mcp_server.py the single entry point. FastMCP supports lifespan= parameter in constructor (asynccontextmanager) — use it to start trickle. For /health: FastMCP exposes a Starlette app via mcp.streamable_http_app() — can add routes to it, or check if FastMCP has a built-in custom_route decorator. The .mcp.json docker exec command stays the same (stdio still works via docker exec -i dotmd dotmd mcp).
